analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 2 finding(s) from 7 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: If dmi_jtag allows jtag_unlock to be asserted without valid hash comparison (e.g., via testmode_i), the entire lock protection is defeated.
findings.vulnerability_category: unlock / write reglk_ctrl / reglk_ctrl lock bits / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: jtag_unlock high clears all lock bits (reglk_mem).
dmi_jtag instantiation shows testmode_i and we_flag_4 are connected, but internal logic unknown.

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: JTAG interface Operation: unlock / write reglk_ctrl Object: reglk_ctrl lock bits Expected guard: jtag_unlock must be authenticated via hash comparison; no bypass via testmode or debug Observed behavior: In reglk_wrapper, jtag_unlock is used to reset the lock memory: ~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9) causes reglk_mem to be cleared. Thus asserting jtag_unlock unlocks all peripherals. The jtag_unlock signal originates from dmi_jtag, which receives testmode_i and we_flag_4. The RTL of dmi_jtag is not available in the provided files. Path: dmi_jtag -> jtag_unlock -> reglk_wrapper reset condition. Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: The security of the entire lock mechanism depends on the robustness of jtag_unlock generation within dmi_jtag (not inspected).
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Internal logic of dmi_jtag module is not available; it is unknown whether testmode_i or debug_mode_i can override the hash comparison to assert jtag_unlock without authentication.

findings.summary: Multiple crypto peripherals (aes0, aes2, hmac) do not clear key registers on reset, leaving sensitive data possibly exposed. The lock bit register (reglk_ctrl) defaults to unlocked, giving a window of opportunity before software locks are set.
findings.vulnerability_category: reset / key registers, lock bits, state registers / permission-related RTL security issue

evidence.description: Reset does not clear key0, key1, key2 arrays. They are left uninitialized.
Similarly, aes2_wrapper reset misses key arrays.
HMAC wrapper resets data array but not key0, ikey_hash_bytes, okey_hash_bytes.
Lock bits are cleared on reset (unlocked state) unless jtag_unlock is asserted (which also clears them).

findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: system reset (rst_ni and derivatives) Operation: reset Object: key registers, lock bits, state registers Expected guard: keys reset to zero; lock bits reset to locked state Observed behavior: In aes0_wrapper, reset clears start, p_c, and state but does not clear key0, key1, key2 arrays. In aes2_wrapper, similarly, keys are not cleared. In hmac_wrapper, key0, ikey_hash_bytes, and okey_hash_bytes are not cleared on reset. The reglk_wrapper resets reglk_mem to 0 (unlocked). Path: always_ff blocks with reset: only partial state cleared; key arrays left uninitialized. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Key material may persist across resets and be accessible after a warm reset; lock default unlocked leaves all peripherals unprotected until software locks them, opening a window of exposure.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
